sentences.text: Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
Yeah, sure.
So I think I finished by talking about the bunkers.
We were 10 in one floor.
We were 1,000 in a barrack.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lunchtime, we had to be all in our places, in our barracks.
We got one huge pot where they put 10 portions of whatever we had for lunch-- kind of a soup.
We didn't have a plate.
We didn't have a bowl.
[SIGHS] But we had to eat.
So we just took that pot, and each of us drink-- drunk from the pot, and we counted the gulps-- how much one is swallowing-- 1, 2, 3.
It was very important that one shouldn't have more or less.
And that's how the pot went from hand to hand.
Now, how-- how did we feel?
How did we feel they didn't give a plate-- a bowl?
But that's how it was.
And of course, the daily call, the call-- roll call-- roll call, the daily roll call, that was-- I don't-- I never understand why that was so important.
We had to get up at 4 o'clock in the morning.
The stars were already at-- on the skies-- rain, wind, whatever weather it was-- out from the barrack.
They were chasing out-- and stood there in line for hours and hours, because we were a lot.
We were five in a row.
And the-- the blockalteste-- that's how the-- the lady was called who was in charge with us.
And she had to give a very precise count-- how many we are.
Because in the daytime, we might change from one place to the other.
So that's why we had to-- we had to be numbered-- not numbered-- counted, actually-- for hours and hours-- hours and hours.
And-- and-- and our-- with our bald heads, you see, we were cold-- and whatever dresses we had-- there was not a paper to grab-- nothing.
Oh, I didn't talk about that.
That took-- that took about four hours in the morning and about three hours in the evening.
Two times a day they had to count us-- two times a day.
Auschwitz-- the life in Auschwitz was absolutely below human-- whatever.
You-- you know, I-- I think even the animal lived better.
We didn't have absolute, absolute nothing, you know what means.
We had a-- we had a bathroom for everybody.
The bathroom, you go in.
You went about, I don't know, 200, 300 people the same time-- the same time, like a table with rows-- two, three such huge thing.
But we didn't have men there.
Sometimes, men were working there.
It didn't matter.
It didn't matter.
We-- we lost that kind of thing.
It didn't matter a man or a woman.
But we didn't have to do it with men too much.
We didn't have a piece of paper-- absolute, absolute nothing, nothing, nothing, nothing.
[SIGHS]
I was 24.
From time to time-- you see, Auschwitz was a place actually, where people changed.
Because people came, and they took people to-- to work.
But they never knew who.
They had only numbers, you see.
I was there, for instance, for months.
And some others were there just from one day to the other.
But there were-- people were always changing.
And somehow, I-- I got the job in my block-- we call it a block-- barrack, my barrack-- as a cleaning girl.
You see, we have to wash the floor, sweep the floor, such things, which was a very, very good thing, because I didn't have to stay for that long outside.
While the people were outside, we were cleaning.
We were many of us.
They made a selection every couple of weeks.
The selection was made always by doctors-- German officers-- doctors.
Then, it was the so-called blocksperre, when the-- when the barrack was closed-- all the barracks.
Nobody could get out.
And then, we had to undress-- naked, completely naked-- and go one by one.
The-- the-- the German doctor stood by the barrack door-- out.
And he looked at us.
We tried to pinch our faces to look-- to look red-- to look better.
And he just touched our muscles, and then he looked at us.
So whoever had the slightest sign on-- on their body, you see, or whoever looked very thin, or sick, they were right away selected for the crematorium.
We know that by that time.
The other ones passed.
But that was always a terrible, tragic thing.
Because those who were selected, they knew already what's going to happen.
I had with me a very dear first cousin who was there with her sister, you see?
The sister was only about 12, but she was a grown-up.
And somehow, by some miracle, she-- she managed to-- got to the-- between the older ones.
So she was with her sister.
But she was a spoiled little girl.
And she didn't like the food.
She didn't want to eat.
She was sickly.
She got selected.
She cried.
She screamed.
I don't think she understood what's going on.
She was a child.
But-- but she wanted her sister, who replaced her mother, actually.
And the sister, she-- a 20-year-old robust, healthy country girl-- she went with her.
She went with her.
I can't believe that.
I can't believe that she went with her.
And she died with her.
She knew what's going to happen.
She was so brave.
You see what people did?
It's-- it's really unbelievable.
Somehow, I always went through all the-- I-- I told you before.
But even in Auschwitz, I looked good.
I-- I I was with a friend, which we called-- like a sister.
I had with me three cousins.
We were a group of 10.
We kept together all the time, all the time.
We managed to-- to live in such terrible circumstances, but we lived through in Auschwitz til September.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were very skinny, and we were always hungry, but we didn't work there somehow.
Because this was just a-- a changing place.
So that was actually called Birkenau.
I find that out just a couple of years ago.
I was there.
I went back there.
And I don't know if-- if I can remember.
That little girl, yes, she slept near me, you know, we-- if-- if one had to turn, everybody had to turn.
When she couldn't sleep, she couldn't sleep.
She was a very young.
And-- and I ask her, why are you moving so much?
I can't sleep.
What's the matter with you?
She says, you see, when I went to sleep at home, I always used to play with my hair-- to twist my hair like this.
And I don't have hair now.
So she couldn't sleep, poor girl.
Yeah.
And that was the other girl.
Oh, I have to remember things.
When-- when we had to undress-- I'm going back now a little bit-- there was also a young girl.
She had a-- she had a little package in her hand.
And that was her insulin.
She was diabetic.
She couldn't live without that.
We had to throw away everything.
She says, I want to keep this.
This is my life.
It keeps me alive.
She-- they didn't let her.
They didn't let her.
We had to leave everything, everything, everything.
She died, poor girl.
She died a short while after.
I-- I remember her very well.
Some people knew-- some people.
I don't know how, because the blockalteste, you know, she was in touch with the Germans, I guess.
They must have a calendar.
But-- but we know when it was Tisha B'Av.
It was Tisha B'Av, and we were there.
And you wouldn't believe it, but we were fasting.
We did the-- we got-- we had to take the food.
We got that pot of food and put it aside in our bunk-- bunker, you know?
And we didn't eat.
We kept our piece of bread.
We didn't eat.
Yeah.
And you had it after sundown?
Yeah.
Yeah, we kept it, yeah.
You, see?
We still-- we still-- we were-- we were-- we were brought up very religiously, and we still believed that-- that God is there somewhere.
And he might help us to get out from here, which he did, actually-- for some of us, anyways-- for some of us.
Auschwitz was a terrible, terrible place.
You see, those selections, those were so tragic.
You know, to see that they made a chain from human beings, you know, holding hands in-- in-- a-- a-- a chain around.
And in that chain, they put those who were taken to the crematoriums-- not the crematoriums-- first to the gas chambers.
And those-- we-- all the people-- we had to-- I wasn't there, anyway.
But it-- that doesn't matter.
I don't want to excuse myself.
You did everything that you had to do there.
There was-- there was no other way.
You have to survive, that's all.
And I saw among those people-- I-- I still see their faces.
One of them was a girlfriend of mine who wasn't with me together.
But she was always very thin, just by nature, very, very, very thin.
Now, over there, she didn't have what to eat, so she became like my finger.
She was selected.
And I saw some-- some others.
And they knew already, you see.
If you don't know, it's different.
They knew already.
From-- from June-- from June-- Shavuot-- around-- til about September.
Some people knew the-- the days, even, you see.
But-- the year?
Oh, the year is from-- from '44.
The year was '44.
The year I know.
I know the months.
I don't know the days.
But some people know the days, too.
We were-- we were again, selected.
The hearty one went to-- away.
And the-- the-- the sick ones went to-- and then once such a selection, we were sent to another-- to a concentration camp-- to Guben.
You see?
Guben was-- as I found out later-- about 200 kilometers from Berlin.
It became the East Berlin later.
It was a small concentration camp where we made war radios-- I think I may call it that way-- radios which was put in airplanes, and tanks, and all over.
They had them by the hundreds.
I don't know why they needed so much-- by the hundreds.
We made-- we made 200 radios every day-- 200-- maybe more-- thousands.
That was a-- a-- assembly work, which I never saw, you know, with the band-- move in the middle.
We pushed.
They teached us.
They taught us how to do it.
And we learned very fast-- very, very fast.
The lager was very small-- of course, fenced.
But-- but we had, each of us, a bed-- a bed, yeah-- with a blanket.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had a bowl.
And we had a piece of soap.
We never had that-- and a-- a towel, also, a small bathroom.
We had that.
We had one dress, just what we had on us.
Sometimes, I think, oh, how was it?
But-- but someday, when we didn't work, we washed it, and we dried it somehow.
I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was one hour going to the factory-- the factory was called Florence.
I remember the factory's name-- and one hour coming back.
We worked 12 hours a day.
We had a 1/2 an hour lunch time.
Then, they gave us later, only the later months, some kind of a soup.
We got the food when we came home from the factory, as it was day and night shift.
I worked a couple of months daytime and then a couple of months nighttime.
Somehow, I liked better the nighttime.
The night went faster than the day.
We were always hungry-- always hungry-- constantly.
And when we had a little leisure time, we talked about food-- how you cook this, how you cook this, oh, my mother made that or what.
Guben wasn't so terrible but we were, of course, prisoners.
We were-- I-- we went to the-- to the factory in the middle of the street, escorted by the German Nazi-- whatever.
I-- I watched the people going on the sidewalks leisurely dressed well-- we weren't dressed-- dressed well-- pushing the children's swing.
I-- I remember thinking that, is it going to be a time whenever I will go free on the sidewalk like them?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Work was very fast.
We had to work very fast.
It-- it was-- you know, they timed it.
The-- the machine has to be pushed on the table every three minutes.
In that three minutes, you have to finish whatever you had to do.
But we were good.
They-- they told us that we were better than the group-- they had of some other kind of people.
And-- and well, there were some German womans-- woman among us, just for control purposes.
We-- we weren't allowed to talk to them.
[SIGHS]
It was a cold winter.
But when Christmas came, they-- they put in our drawers some-- some little things, which was just such a warming thought in this rigid environment, you know?
I got a pair of stockings.
It was December, you know?
We didn't have-- who gave us stockings?
Nobody had-- or a pair of gloves, a shawl-- something.
Yeah, well, they were human beings.
They-- they knew that we weren't criminals.
They-- they knew.
And they had also-- they had saw us in the army.
It was-- it was [NON-ENGLISH].
They didn't have too much in there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Guben, we kept ourselves quite well-- very thin, very hungry, but we weren't sick, you see.
And-- because-- because we were treated like-- like-- we were needed, you see.
We-- it-- it sounds ironic.
But we, the enemy, had Hitler to keep up the bar with our work.
We worked by the thousands.
We were all over.
And I remember once some-- some girls tried to escape, because sometimes, you had to go at nighttime to-- to bring the bread.
And it was dark.
And if somebody wanted, they can-- to-- maybe to-- to get away.
But they brought them back right away.
Our clothes was made in that way-- our coat-- that it has a big patch sewed in in the middle of the back-- sewed in, not painted-- which was in stripes.
So from far away, you could see that this is a prisoner.
They took him back.
They were-- they were punished.
Yeah, but very fast.
First of all, we weren't so many.
In Guben, we were only 1,000 altogether-- altogether.
In Auschwitz, we were 3-- 30,000.
And then we had to go.
We had to work.
So yeah.
They-- they-- they counted us every day, going and coming.
But it was-- it was made very, very, very, very fast, so-- because there-- there wasn't time.
We-- we needed some time to sleep, to take a rest.

And we needed time to get to the factory in rain, and snow, with our bald heads, you see-- with-- with no stockings, which we-- they just gave us one coat, we had, which was-- whether thin, or-- as it-- as it came out, you know-- not-- not winter-like-- absolutely.
Just we were very young.
In Guben, we-- so nobody died.
There was two girls who were sick, and I think they-- they didn't survive the-- the road from Guben.
You see?
From Guben-- because they told us that the sick ones-- there were always several sick people-- they will-- they are going to come by car, or I don't know.
We walked.
The other ones, they never got there.
So the-- the-- the walk from Guben-- we went to Bergen-Belsen was our next stop, which was the infamous Bergen-Belsen.
Who wasn't in Bergen-Belsen wasn't in a concentration camp.
Bergen-Belsen was a terrible, terrible thing.
I don't know how much time we have to talk about that.
Do we have?
If we have time.
[SIGHS]
--shelters in the factory, yes, sure.
It took a long time till we went down the steps.
It was all over when we got down.
But you wouldn't believe it.
We were laughing.
We liked that.
We loved that sound!
They are going to hit it.
The war is going to-- we weren't afraid to dying.
It's so interesting.
When we have nothing to lose, it looks like you-- I-- I don't know how.
Nobody was afraid.
We were laughing-- very careful not to laugh loud.
They shouldn't hear it.
You see, we weren't afraid at all.
They took us down.
We had to go down.
And then, one day, we didn't go to work.
It was January.
That was '45 already.
We didn't go to what's happening.
We didn't know what's happening.
We still hear the bombardment, which meant the enemy is coming.
We were-- there were all kind of rumors-- the war is going to be over.
The war is going to be over.
[SIGHS]
There were some people.
We had a-- the next lager was a French lager.
There were only men.
And they were-- they were more informed about what's going on outside.
And then, some of the girls knew how to talk to them.
And that's how we find out certain things which was 1/2 true, or how knows?
Really, we didn't know what's going on.
So one day, they gave us 1/2 a bread, a piece of margarine, I think-- a blanket.
Off we went by foot.
It's called a march-- by foot-- the winter, the snow.
They never gave us anything of help.
We took-- I took out the-- the lining from my coat, and I made myself a-- some-- something for my-- a turban or something for my head, you see?
We didn't have nothing, nothing-- never mind stockings, or gloves, or-- So we walked about two, three weeks, from the morning til the evening.
In the evening, they put us in a stall with straw, you see-- in-- in a village somewhere.
Of course, many people couldn't.
We were already-- we were already drawn out.
We were already skinny.
We didn't have-- we weren't that-- that-- that healthy anymore.
I mean, we might be healthy, but not strong enough.
And some people just couldn't take the walk.
That's all.
What do you think?
They were shot-- left there in the snow-- left in the snow.
You couldn't do nothing.
Thank God nobody very close in-- in-- in my branch.
It didn't happen to nobody who was very close to me.
And then we-- we-- they put us in a train for a day or two.
And then we arrived to-- Bergen-Belsen.
Belsen, Bergen-Belsen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Bergen-Belsen is something undescribable.
First of all, they accumulated 10 times as many people as there were room for.
I don't know why that was the centrum for that.
It was in a terrible, terrible shape.
Auschwitz was a cooler place, according to me.
Auschwitz was clean-- kept clean.
We-- we never had a lice on us or anything.
We had water to drink and to wash ourselves.
That's good.
But we were able to wash ourselves.
We didn't have soap.
But anyway-- but Bergen-Belsen, there hardly was food.
But it was the end already.
And they took too many people there.
And there was no place.
We were in a-- we were put in a corridor-- sat on the floor, again, like with a-- with our knees to our chest.
But there were-- it was packed!
The toilet was overrun.
There was no water to wash or to drink anything.
We got a tea in the morning.
We got some kind of a food which didn't have any taste-- a little, just sometimes.
We didn't get-- it was disorganized already, absolutely!
And then, then the tragedy came that the typhus-- typhus-- typhoid-- typhus breakout.
Lice-- first of all, it's sad to tell.
Lice is something.
I think if I would see a lice now, I will die.
Lice was the most, most, most terrible thing in the concentration camp.
You couldn't do anything about it.
You will absolutely have it.
First, when we got-- we're very clean when we got there.
And then mixing with them, sleeping with them on the floor, whatever, of course, we got it.
And then, we tried to clean ourselves a little bit.
But-- but it came to a point where there's-- where nothing helps.
Nothing helped.
We took off our dresses, and we-- we-- went-- the floor was our place, anyway.
We went to sleep without anything, because then-- then-- then we could just sweep off the lice, you know?
We didn't have-- it didn't bother us so much.
We lost weight.
When I saw-- you see, we saw ourselves in the other one.
Because there wasn't a glass or a mirror.

When I saw a friend of mine with whom I was in Guben, after just three, four weeks, I couldn't recognize her-- just the two big bones-- bones all over-- bones over-- bones here-- just bones, just bones.
I got scared.
That's how we looked.
You know, I became flat completely-- flat-- flat like a table.
I always thought-- ah, as was a woman, after all-- I always thought, would I ever have breasts again?
What's-- what's going to-- till nothing, nothing, nothing-- flat-- although, I am bony, anyway.
I had big bones.
Everybody was like that.
Bergen-Belsen-- there, it was dirt, and louse, and sickness, and no food-- nothing.
It was-- it was worse than-- than Dante's-- what is it called?
I don't know that.
It's-- it's a famous-- it's a very famous-- anyway-- purgatory-- Dante's purgatory.
But you-- you--
I was with 10 people always together.
They were my cousins, and they were my closest friends.
One of them-- we became so close-- Elizabeth [NON-ENGLISH]-- so close, that we were like a-- like a role model to everybody, because it was so harmonious our-- our being together-- and so close.
And-- but she said, I'm not going to survive that.
She told me everything you-- my husband is-- I'm sure he's surviving.
And you tell him that this is there.
And that is there, and where we hide certain things, and so.
And I laugh at him.
Look.
I tried to-- to-- to put-- I was the strongest, somehow.
But she survived.
She survived.
But other ones-- I had a cousin, a very close cousin.
She was an exceptional human being-- an angel, you know-- between a couple of millions, you find them-- good-hearted, nice, you know.
And her sister came from another line.
She gave her portion to her, because she looked very bad.
I-- I just told her.
We were all sick.
But even though, they counted us.
We couldn't go out, so we stood in the barrack.
We had to sit up.
And if somebody couldn't sit up, I hold her, my cousin, because she couldn't sit anymore.
She looked at me.
She says, 'Irene, you are so good.'
And I-- I told myself, well, why this?
Why is she saying this?
I couldn't do anything for her.
And the next minute, she was dead.
The next minute, she was dead after that.
Typhus called-- typhus killed people in two days, just like this.
They were dying like-- like flies, you know.
And then, you know what happened?
We picked them up.
And we took them out in front of our-- it was wintertime.
And there was no people enough to take up either dead people.
In our barrack, 50 people died-- one day.
We put them in front over there.
There was a hill of corpses.
And there was one more over there.
And I looked at Roshe-- Roshe-- that was Roshe.
And I didn't cry.
I didn't cry.
I was-- I was wondering, you know.
I-- I-- I felt bad, because of that.
[CRYING] I couldn't die.
I didn't have tears.
Three days later, her sister died.
Meanwhile, we had a friend.
She just-- she just lost her mind.
They took her away.
She just lost her mind somehow in that environment.
No-- no wonder.
You know, we slept on the floor-- the head to the wall.
And the others-- the other row, there was in the middle of the barrack, just a row where to walk.
So one-- one was the-- with the head to the walkway, and the other one with the head to the wall.
Now, of course, the walkway wasn't good, because they can step on you, you see?
So some people gave a piece of bread to change.
I-- I never did that-- to go-- to go-- to take the wall.
But later on, there was no problem, because so many died among us, that that was no problem.
From the 10 people that our group, we survived two.
Everybody died over there in Bergen-Belsen in a couple of weeks.
The typhus killed everybody.
I got sick, too.
I don't know.
I-- by miracle, I survived.
Bergen-Belsen, you-- it's-- you cannot describe.
There is-- you cannot paint it.
You-- you can--
